source.title: AL Solutions Case Study Final Investigation Report July 2014

actions_taken: Employees called 911 after hearing the explosion
Supervisor and plant manager provided aid to injured press operator
New Cumberland Volunteer Fire Department responded to the scene
Water deluge system activated upon detection of high temperatures
Firefighters attempted to access the building but fire was too intense
Airlift transported injured contractor to hospital at approximately 2:30 p.m.
CSB commissioned combustible dust testing of materials from the facility
OSHA West Virginia Area Office conducted inspection and issued one willful and 12 serious violations
AL Solutions agreed to EPA settlement implementing company-wide safeguards in December 2013
event.summary: On December 9, 2010, a metal dust explosion and fire occurred at the AL Solutions facility in New Cumberland, West Virginia, during production operations. The explosion likely initiated when zirconium particulates ignited in a blender due to frictional heating or sparks from metal-to-metal contact between defective blender blades and the blender sidewall. The burning zirconium dust cloud propagated fire to open drums and tubs of titanium and zirconium throughout the production building. Three employees were killed and one electrical contractor was injured. The incident was attributed to inadequate engineering controls, failure to enforce dust management procedures, lack of compliance with NFPA 484, absence of a dust collection system, use of an inappropriate water deluge fire suppression system, and a history of unaddressed mechanical defects in the blender.
recommendations: 2006-1-H-1: Issue a standard designed to prevent combustible dust fires and explosions in general industry, based on NFPA 654 and NFPA 484, including hazard assessment, engineering controls, housekeeping, building design, explosion protection, operating procedures, and worker training.

2011-3-I-WV R1: For all new and existing equipment and operations at AL Solutions facilities that process combustible metal dusts or powders, apply NFPA 484-2012 Chapters 12 (Titanium), 13 (Zirconium), 15 (Fire Prevention, Fire Protection, and Emergency Response), and 16 (Combustible Metal Recycling Facilities).
2011-3-I-WV R2: Develop training materials that address combustible dust hazards and plant-specific metal dust hazards and train all employees and contractors, with periodic refresher training.
2011-3-I-WV R3: Prohibit the use of sprinkler systems and water deluge systems in all buildings that process or store combustible metals.
2011-3-I-WV R4: Conduct a process hazard analysis as defined in NFPA 484-2012, Section 12.2.5, and submit a copy to the local fire department or the enforcing authority for the fire code.

hazards.name: Combustible titanium and zirconium metal dust in production environment
Flammable hydrogen gas generated by water-reactive metal dust

threats.name: Frictional ignition from metal-to-metal contact in defective blender (blades scraping sidewall)
Accumulation and dispersion of combustible metal dust in production building
Blender lid left open during operation allowing dust dispersion
Storage of excess combustible metal drums in production area
Hydrogen gas accumulation from water-reactive metal dust without adequate ventilation

consequences.name: Three employee fatalities from explosion and fire
One contractor injury (severe burns) from fireball
Destruction and thermal damage to production building and equipment
Permanent shutdown of New Cumberland production facility
Fire propagation to open drums and tubs of titanium and zirconium throughout building

controls.name: Blender equipment maintenance and blade adjustment to reduce metal-to-metal contact
controls.barrier_role: Reduce ignition risk from blender blade-to-sidewall contact
controls.lod_basis: Primary engineering control to prevent ignition source

supporting_text: maintenance personnel adjusted the blender blades to increase clearance from the blender wall, but this action did not permanently address the issue with the metal-to-metal contact
maintenance personnel decided to replace parts of the blender in an effort to resolve the issue
The maintenance worker welded this crack, and operators continued to use the blender
On the morning of the incident, an operator requested that maintenance replace a worn paddle in the blender
All of the maintenance performed on the blender only temporarily addressed the problems caused by the metal-to-metal contact

controls.name: Blender lid closure during production to contain dust
controls.barrier_role: Prevent dispersion of combustible metal dust from blender into production atmosphere
controls.lod_basis: Procedural control to limit dust dispersion

supporting_text: the blender lid, intended to be closed during production, was found left in the open position
Management did not enforce this practice at the facility, and the blender, conveyor belt lid, and storage drums were regularly left open during operation

controls.name: Water wash-down housekeeping to control dust accumulation
controls.barrier_role: Remove accumulated combustible metal dust from equipment and surfaces
controls.lod_basis: Secondary dust control measure in absence of dust collection system

supporting_text: AL Solutions regularly washed down the equipment with water as its primary means to eliminate dust from process equipment and areas
AL Solutions instead relied on the use of water as the only method to eliminate dust fires and explosions by keeping dust moist and avoiding dust accumulation on equipment and flat surfaces
AL Solutions did not use a dust collection system to remove zirconium and titanium dusts generated during processing

controls.name: Dust collection system (absent - not installed)
controls.barrier_role: Capture and remove combustible metal dust at source to prevent accumulation and dispersion
controls.lod_basis: Recommended primary engineering control per NFPA 484

supporting_text: AL Solutions did not use a dust collection system to remove zirconium and titanium dusts generated during processing, as recommended by industry standards for the safe handling of combustible metal dusts and powders
AL Solutions did not mitigate the hazards of metal dust explosions through engineering controls, such as a dust collection system
Jamegy did not take the opportunity to install dust collection systems in accordance with the existing NFPA combustible dust codes at that time

controls.name: Restriction of combustible metal storage to warehouse/outside storage (separation from production area)
controls.barrier_role: Limit quantity of flammable metal in production area to reduce fuel load
controls.lod_basis: Procedural control to limit fuel inventory in production area

supporting_text: to limit the quantity of flammable metal in the blending area, only metal currently in production was to be allowed in the production area. All other material was to be kept in a separate warehouse or in outside storage. However, AL Solutions employees commonly left barrels of titanium and zirconium in the production building, even if they were not in use
at the time of the incident, AL Solutions was storing both metals in close proximity in the production building, which fueled the fire and explosion

controls.name: Ignition-proof electrical equipment enclosures in production building
controls.barrier_role: Prevent electrical ignition of combustible metal dust
controls.lod_basis: Engineering control to eliminate electrical ignition sources

supporting_text: The AL Solutions production building equipment had ignition-proof enclosures on motors and electrical wiring to prevent dusts in the production atmosphere from igniting because of electrical arcs and heat produced by moving parts or mechanical impacts
All electrical equipment in the blending and press room was designed to be sealed to prevent a pathway for dust to any electrical sparks or ignition sources

controls.name: Spark-resistant tools and 100% cotton clothing for operators
controls.barrier_role: Reduce ignition risk from static electricity and metal-to-metal contact during manual operations
controls.lod_basis: Administrative control to reduce ignition sources

supporting_text: Operators used spark-resistant tools and wore 100 percent cotton clothes, which, although not flame resistant, were intended to reduce the risk of sparks from metal-to-metal contact or static electricity

controls.name: No open flames and smoking restriction in production building
controls.barrier_role: Eliminate open flame ignition sources from production area
controls.lod_basis: Administrative control to eliminate ignition sources

supporting_text: No open flames were allowed in the production building, and all smoking was restricted to an area outside the break room

controls.name: Hydrogen gas detection system with water deluge activation
controls.barrier_role: Detect elevated hydrogen concentrations and trigger suppression response
controls.lod_basis: Detection and automated response to hydrogen accumulation

supporting_text: The main production building had a water deluge system that was set to activate upon detection of high temperatures or high concentrations of hydrogen gas
Sensors located in the blender and press feed belt conveyor system would activate the deluge of water if readings reached a designated alarm point
OSHA cited AL Solutions for several serious violations of the General Duty Clause, including an inadequate hydrogen detection system
AL Solutions did not have a ventilation system to control hydrogen concentrations

controls.name: Water deluge fire suppression system
controls.barrier_role: Suppress fire in production building upon activation
controls.lod_basis: Automated fire suppression system - activated post-explosion

supporting_text: By the time the VFD arrived, the building water deluge system had activated

NFPA 484 declared that automatic sprinkler protection shall not be permitted in areas where combustible metals are produced or handled because water sprayed on a titanium or zirconium fire can lead to burning metal dust or an explosion
The installation of a deluge system to control a metal dust fire was contrary to recognized industry practices and to the titanium and zirconium MSDSs for extinguishing metal dust fires
The willful violation issued to AL Solutions cited the use of water rather than the recommended sand or salt extinguishing agents to suppress a metal dust fire

controls.name: Emergency evacuation plan and fire department notification procedure
controls.barrier_role: Protect personnel and initiate emergency response upon fire or explosion
controls.lod_basis: Emergency response procedure to limit personnel harm

supporting_text: the AL Solutions safety manual instructed operators to evacuate the building, alert management, and call the fire department
employees in the main office building, who heard the explosion, called 911 to request emergency assistance
The supervisor, an electrical contractor, and the plant manager provided aid to the press operator until emergency personnel arrived

controls.name: Flame-resistant clothing (PPE) for operators
controls.barrier_role: Reduce severity of burn injuries to personnel from flash fire

supporting_text: NFPA 484 requires the use of flame-resistant clothing to reduce the severity of injuries from flash fires. AL Solutions provided its operators with 100 percent cotton work shirts and pants that were not flame resistant

Other serious violations included the lack of training, poor communication about hazards, and failure to provide employees with fire-resistant clothing to reduce the severity of injuries from a flash fire

controls.name: Safety audits and process hazard analysis
controls.barrier_role: Identify and address metal dust explosion hazards through systematic review
controls.lod_basis: Management system control to identify and correct hazards

supporting_text: The CSB found that facility safety and insurance audits did not adequately identify and address metal dust hazards at the New Cumberland facility
A comprehensive process hazard analysis was not recommended or performed despite the hazardous nature of the process and despite previous incidents
Neither of the insurance audits made any recommendations to AL Solutions that it should change its process design or dust management systems
In both 1998 and 2006, the consultant recommended that Jamegy acquire an automatic fire control system for the blender and press equipment

controls.name: Combustible dust training and hazard communication for employees and contractors
controls.barrier_role: Ensure personnel awareness of combustible dust hazards and safe operating practices

supporting_text: The CSB determined that the operators, supervisors, and engineers at AL Solutions were not familiar with NFPA 484
Other serious violations included the lack of training, poor communication about hazards
CSB investigators learned through interviews that AL Solutions employees were generally aware that the metal dust was combustible and that a spark on the metal could cause a fire

controls.name: Management enforcement of safety procedures and dust management policies
controls.barrier_role: Ensure compliance with established dust control and safe operating procedures

supporting_text: the CSB found that management did not enforce these requirements
Management did not enforce this practice at the facility, and the blender, conveyor belt lid, and storage drums were regularly left open during operation
management did not enforce company policies to reduce the risk of metal dust explosions and fires, and its dust management practices did not align with industry consensus standards, such as NFPA 484

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.

pif_ids_referenced.PIF-001: Competence - operators and engineers not familiar with NFPA 484; inadequate hazard recognition training
pif_ids_referenced.PIF-002: Situational awareness - maintenance personnel aware of recurring blender defects but did not adequately assess ignition risk
pif_ids_referenced.PIF-003: Supervision/procedures - management did not enforce established safety procedures (lid closure, storage separation, PPE)
pif_ids_referenced.PIF-004: Safety culture - management did not act on previous incidents or audit recommendations; no process hazard analysis conducted
pif_ids_referenced.PIF-005: Training - lack of training on combustible dust hazards and NFPA 484 cited as OSHA violation
